text: This video depicts a robotic bilateral transversus abdominis release for a large midline incisional hernia. The patient was a sixty-nine-year-old female who presented to clinic with a large, painful midline incisional hernia. She had previously undergone open partial colectomy in two thousand and fourteen. Her medical history also included a BMI of thirty-three, hypothyroidism, and hypertension. A CT scan was performed which revealed a bowel containing hernia eight centimeters in width, and fourteen centimeters in length. The rectus muscle measured approximately eight centimeters in diameter. Following an informed discussion in clinic, we elected to proceed with bilateral transverse abdominis release.

The surgeon demonstrates port placement, initially entering the abdomen with a 5mm trocar at the left subcostal margin. Two additional 8mm trocars are placed, one at the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) and another laterally. The 5mm port is upsized to 8mm, and the XI robot is docked. Long trocars are used for arm clearance. Right-sided ports are placed midway through the procedure after completing the right-sided transversus abdominis release (TAR). Lysis of adhesions is performed for 90 minutes to reduce the small bowel from the hernia sac and clear the abdominal wall.

The surgeon incises the linea alba along the hernia margin, identifying the right rectus muscle and entering the retrorectus space. The dissection progresses superiorly and inferiorly, revealing the epigastric vessels on the posterior aspect of the rectus muscle. Lateral dissection reaches the neurovascular bundle, where a fascial incision is made to expose the transversus abdominis muscle. The muscle fibers are divided using scissor electrocautery, with careful elevation of the muscle while preserving the transversalis fascia. The myofascial release extends superiorly to the diaphragm and inferiorly into the preperitoneal space in the pelvis.

The surgeon dissects the cord structures in the pelvis, with the potential for concurrent inguinal hernia repair if present. A wide flap is developed laterally, allowing for extensive mobilization of the right posterior elements. The bare bloody muscle is retracted anteriorly. Three robotic eight millimeter trocars are placed on the right side, just lateral to the semilunar line, which will later be covered by retromuscular mesh.

The surgeon measures the dissected half of the abdomen and cuts a 26-centimeter wide mesh for retromuscular placement. Three tacking sutures are placed on the opposite abdominal wall to secure the mesh. The robot is undocked, the arms are repositioned, and new ports are redocked. Dissection begins on the left side, joining the preperitoneal space inferiorly and entering the retrorectus space, which is developed superiorly.

The surgeon performs a transverse abdominis muscle release on the lateral side, mirroring the technique used on the contralateral side. A 12-inch, 2-0 reverse barb suture is inserted, and the posterior rectus sheath is closed in a running fashion without tension.

The surgeon closes the hernia defect using a running number one reverse barbed suture, sequentially tightening the sutures with low insufflation pressure. Quilting sutures are applied to assist with plication of the hernia sac and reduce seroma formation. The mesh is unfurled and placed in the retromuscular space, broadly overlapping the closed midline defect without fixation.

All eight millimeter port sites were covered with the placement of the mesh and did not require fascial closure. The patient did very well after surgery. She was discharged to home on post-operative day two. She was noted to be healing well at her follow-up clinic appointment, and no issues were reported one year after surgery.
